findings.title: Datum-shift axis rotation has the wrong sign: velocity, covariance, IMM means and particles are rotated by +gamma where the true ENU-to-ENU transform is -gamma, making the shift worse than not rotating at all
findings.description: datumAxisRotation(old,new) builds R(+gamma) with gamma = delta_lon*sin(mean_lat), and DatumShift.cpp:39/73 applies v_new = R*v_old (also to the 4x4 covariance block, IMM mode means/covariances, and particle velocities). The correct components transform between two tangent-plane bases is M = R_new_ecef2enu * R_old_ecef2enu^T, whose horizontal block is R(-gamma). Verified numerically by replicating Datum::toEnu (exact R*(x-o) form, Datum.cpp:24) in an independent script: for old=(60N,0E), new=(60N,1E), a due-east old-frame velocity (1,0) has true new-frame components (0.99985, -0.01511); the code produces (+0.01511) — sign flipped, total error 2*gamma. Pole sanity check agrees (at lat 90, old-east must map to due SOUTH in a frame 90 deg east; code gives due north). The unit test RotatesVelocityByConvergenceAngle (tests/tracking/test_datum_shift.cpp:47-65) pins the code's own +gamma convention rather than deriving it from reprojection, so it encodes the bug instead of catching it; the scenario test only asserts positions (which go through exact geodetic reprojection and are unaffected). The same flipped R is used by MhtTracker::onDatumRecentered (MhtTracker.cpp:850/857) for every tree node, and by toGeodeticWithCov (core/output/TrackOutput.cpp:17) for the output covariance rotation. A one-line sign fix in datumAxisRotation corrects all call sites consistently.
findings.failure_scenario: Vessel transits >30 km with default auto-recenter (DatumRecenterPolicy.enable_auto_recenter=true). At the recenter, every track's velocity is rotated 2*gamma away from truth instead of 0 (about 0.94 deg at 60N, 3 deg at 80N; larger if a consumer raises recenter_threshold_km). Coasting tracks then propagate with a cross-track velocity error (10 m/s * sin(1 deg) ~ 0.17 m/s), and all tracks emit a simultaneous wrong-direction innovation spike right after each recenter; output COG is biased until the filter washes it out. Covariance ellipses and IMM mode means get the same wrong-direction rotation.
findings.evidence: AxisRotation.cpp:15-22 (gamma and R(+gamma)); DatumShift.cpp:14-30,39-46 (v <- R*v, cov <- Rbar*cov*Rbar^T); independent numeric check: true new-frame velocity of old-frame (1,0) is (0.9998477, -0.0151142), code yields (0.9998858, +0.0151144); test_datum_shift.cpp:57 asserts atan2(R(1,0),R(0,0)) == +gamma (pins the flipped convention).
findings.suggested_fix: Negate gamma in datumAxisRotation (gamma = -delta_lon_rad*sin(mean_lat_rad)) or return R.transpose(); update the two assertions in tests/tracking/test_datum_shift.cpp to the reprojection-derived sign, and add a test that derives velocity by finite-differencing two positions through both datums.

findings.title: recordMiss promotes never-confirmed Tentative tracks to Coasting, which makes one-hit clutter tracks eligible for operator-facing collision-risk events that Tentative status is supposed to gate out
findings.description: recordMiss unconditionally sets tracks_[i].status = TrackStatus::Coasting for any track below the delete threshold, without checking the prior status. The output contract (docs/output-contract.md:25) defines Coasting as 'was Confirmed, now propagated through a detection gap' — a Tentative track that misses must stay Tentative (or die), never become Coasting. CpaEvaluator (core/collision/CpaEvaluator.cpp:26-30) treats Coasting as CPA-eligible while excluding Tentative by default (evaluate_tentative=false, CpaEvaluator.hpp:23). So the status gate is inverted for unconfirmed tracks: excluded while they are receiving hits, included as soon as they miss. No test covers a Tentative track receiving a miss (tests/tracking/test_track_manager.cpp only exercises the Confirmed->Coasting path).
findings.failure_scenario: Tracker(miss_timeout=5s) + TrackManager(confirm_hits=3, delete_misses=3) + CpaEvaluator with defaults. A single clutter radar return at t=0 births Tentative track T (hits=1, TrackManager.cpp:15). T never associates again; at t>5s the stale sweep (Tracker.cpp:192) calls recordMiss(T): misses=1 < 3, status set to Coasting. The next CpaEvaluator::evaluate(t) passes T through the status gate; if own-ship's course passes near the clutter position, probability_below_threshold crosses enter_probability and an Entered collision-risk event fires to the operator sink — a false collision alarm sourced from one unconfirmed clutter blip. T stays CPA-eligible for delete_misses-1 further cycles. Consumers reading TrackOutput.status also see a contract-violating Coasting for a track that was never Confirmed.
findings.evidence: TrackManager.cpp:44-65 (no prior-status check before line 64); CpaEvaluator.cpp:26-30 (status_ok includes Coasting, excludes Tentative when evaluate_tentative=false); CpaEvaluatorConfig.evaluate_tentative default false (CpaEvaluator.hpp:23); docs/output-contract.md:25 defines Coasting as 'was Confirmed'.
findings.suggested_fix: In recordMiss, only demote to Coasting when the track is currently Confirmed (or Coasting); leave Tentative tracks Tentative: if (tracks_[i].status == TrackStatus::Confirmed) tracks_[i].status = TrackStatus::Coasting;

findings.title: A Coasting (ex-Confirmed) track that re-associates is not restored to Confirmed until confirm_hits consecutive fresh hits, contradicting the documented Coasting semantics, and the eventual re-promotion re-fires onTrackConfirmed despite the port's 'fires once' contract
findings.description: recordMiss resets hits to 0 (line 48); on recovery each recordHit increments hits but the status stays Coasting until hits >= confirm_hits_ (line 36). During recovery the track IS receiving fresh estimator updates (Tracker calls recordHit only after a successful update), yet its status remains Coasting — the output contract defines Coasting as 'propagated through a detection gap WITHOUT a fresh update' (docs/output-contract.md:25). Consumers that filter output on status==Confirmed (app/example.cpp:141, app/mht_fusion_example.cpp:163, adapters/foxglove/FoxgloveDebugRecorder.cpp:102 is_output, core/benchmark/BenchRunner.cpp:107) drop an actively-updated confirmed vessel from output for confirm_hits-1 update cycles after every single miss event. When re-promotion finally happens, was_unconfirmed is true (Coasting != Confirmed) so onTrackConfirmed fires a second time for the same track, violating ITrackSink.hpp:40-41 ('fires once when Tentative -> Confirmed') — consumers that allocate UI entries or dedup alerts on first confirmation double-fire.
findings.failure_scenario: confirm_hits=3, AIS track Confirmed and steady. One dropout longer than miss_timeout: recordMiss -> Coasting, hits=0. AIS resumes at 10 s intervals: for the next 2 reports (20 s) the track is updated every cycle but stays Coasting — a Confirmed-filtered operator display blanks the vessel for 20 s during normal AIS class-B behavior. On the 3rd report the sink receives a duplicate onTrackConfirmed event for a track it already registered.
findings.evidence: TrackManager.cpp:30-42 (hits reset by miss at :48, promotion gate at :36, refire via was_unconfirmed at :35-40); ITrackSink.hpp:26-27,40-41 ('fires once when Tentative -> Confirmed'); docs/output-contract.md:25 (Coasting = no fresh update); Confirmed-only filters at FoxgloveDebugRecorder.cpp:102, example.cpp:141, BenchRunner.cpp:107. No test exercises hit-after-miss recovery.
findings.suggested_fix: In recordHit, restore Confirmed immediately when the track's status is Coasting and it was previously Confirmed (track the was-confirmed bit or treat Coasting as a Confirmed substate), and fire onTrackConfirmed only on the first Tentative->Confirmed transition.

findings.title: Documented 'no sensor specs' pattern is a silent no-op for the range/bearing builders: applyDefaultsIfEmpty can never fire on their output, so following the example yields a zero/GPS-only measurement covariance that is silently 100x+ overconfident
findings.description: buildBearingMeasurement always assigns m.covariance = proj.cov (core/types/MeasurementBuilders.cpp:81) — a real 2x2 even when range_std_m and bearing_std_rad are 0 (then cov = sigma_gps^2*I, or exactly zero when position_std_m is also 0). applyDefaultsIfEmpty only fills when covariance.size()==0 (core/types/SensorDefaults.cpp:73), and SensorDefaults has no (ArpaTtm|EoIr, Position2D) entry anyway (SensorDefaults.cpp:31-48) — the arpa_ttm_range_bearing/eoir_range_bearing defaults key on RangeBearing2D, a model the builders never emit (they emit Position2D, MeasurementBuilders.cpp:34). Yet app/example.cpp:124-125 explicitly instructs: 'If your radar didn't report std values, leave them 0 and call applyDefaultsIfEmpty(m, defaults)', and CLAUDE.md:122 states the same generic pattern. The recommended call is a guaranteed no-op, covariance_is_default stays false (no diagnostic), and the degenerate R proceeds.
findings.failure_scenario: A consumer with a spec-less radar follows example.cpp: range_std=0, bearing_std=0, GPS sigma 5 m. Every TTM measurement carries R = 25*I m^2 instead of the intended pessimistic defaults (75 m range sigma -> 5625 m^2): 225x overconfident, silently. Tracks initiate with a near-zero position covariance, gating against subsequent honest sensors collapses, and no flag warns (covariance_is_default=false). If GPS sigma is also 0, R is exactly zero: initiation is silently rejected by the strict PSD check (MeasurementModels.hpp:89) so the sensor never births a track, while matched updates to existing tracks apply R=0 and snap tracks onto raw returns.
findings.evidence: MeasurementBuilders.cpp:71-81 (covariance always set on the success path); SensorDefaults.cpp:72-78 (empty-only fill) and :31-48 (no Position2D entry for ArpaTtm/EoIr); app/example.cpp:124-125 (the no-op advice); CLAUDE.md:122; adapters/ArpaAdapter.hpp:26-28 has its own nonzero defaults so only direct builder consumers are exposed.
findings.suggested_fix: Either have the bearing builders consult a SensorDefaults (or their doc demand nonzero stds and assert/flag zero sigmas), or fix the guidance in example.cpp/CLAUDE.md/integration guide to say: pass the defaults' sigma_range_m/sigma_bearing_rad as builder arguments — and add (sensor, Position2D) awareness or a covariance_is_default marker for this path.

findings.title: ClutterMapSensorDetectionModel caches learned clutter cells in ENU grid coordinates but implements no IDatumChangeSink and offers no shift/clear API, so after an auto-datum recenter the learned lambda map silently applies ~30 km off and stale hot cells persist indefinitely
findings.description: position_maps_ keys cells by floor(ENU/cell_size) in the working datum frame (ClutterMapDetectionModel.cpp:157-161), and paramsFor(z) interpolates at z.value's current-frame ENU (cpp:96-107). The class implements only ISensorDetectionModel; unlike StaticObstacleModel/CoastlineModel/LiveOccupancyModel (which CLAUDE.md's auto-datum section requires registering as datum sinks precisely because they cache ENU state), there is no onDatumRecentered handling and no way to register one. After OwnShipProvider auto-recenters (default policy, 30 km threshold), every learned cell is anchored to the old frame while queries and observe() feeds arrive in the new frame. The class doc itself notes cells unvisited for a long time 'keep their last estimate — persistent map memory', so mis-anchored hot cells (up to max_ratio=64x baseline) are applied at wrong geographic locations forever unless coincidentally overwritten. The bench Sweep's fixed-datum usage is the known exception; a library consumer wiring the decorator into MhtTracker (the shipped imm_cv_ct_mht_cmap wiring pattern) with default auto-recenter hits this.
findings.failure_scenario: Consumer wraps their sensor table in ClutterMapSensorDetectionModel for an MhtTracker, auto-recenter on. Ship transits 35 km past a cluttered shoreline; the map learns 64x-baseline cells over the shore. Datum recenters; the tracker's tracks are shifted (MhtTracker::onDatumRecentered) but the clutter cells are not: the 64x lambda region now sits over open water ~30 km from the real shore in the new frame, suppressing legitimate new-target scores there (higher log lambda_C penalty per MHT branch score), while the actual shoreline reverts to baseline. No error, no diagnostic; cells in the phantom region persist until new returns happen to land in those exact cells.
findings.evidence: ClutterMapDetectionModel.hpp:169-196 (Cell/PositionMap keyed on int64 ENU indices; class implements only ISensorDetectionModel); grep shows no IDatumChangeSink/onDatumRecentered/clear anywhere in the class; CLAUDE.md auto-datum section lists the three other ENU-caching models as mandatory datum sinks; MhtTracker.cpp:846-860 shifts tracks but detection_model_ is untouched.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement IDatumChangeSink on ClutterMapSensorDetectionModel: on recenter, either rebuild cell keys via the old->new reprojection (cell centers through Datum) or, simplest and safe, clear all maps (tau=20 s relearns quickly); document the wiring in the integration guide alongside the other datum sinks.

findings.title: 5-state (IMM CV/CT mainline) covariance shift rotates only the top-left 4x4 block, leaving the omega row/column cross-covariances in the old frame's axes
findings.description: rotateCovarianceInPlace transforms cov.topLeftCorner<4,4>() with blkdiag(R,R) but the shipped IMM estimator uses 5-state [px,py,vx,vy,omega] tracks with 5x5 covariances (ImmEstimator.cpp:448-467); the CT mode's Jacobian couples omega into vx/vy so P(0:3,4) becomes nonzero in operation. The correct transform is blkdiag(R,R,1)*P*blkdiag(R,R,1)^T, which also rotates the 4x1 cross-covariance column P(0:3,4) by blkdiag(R,R). Leaving it untouched makes the shifted matrix not a similarity transform of the original: cross-correlations between turn rate and position/velocity point ~gamma (~0.5-1.5 deg per recenter) off relative to the rotated block, and in the near-singular high-correlation case the result can lose positive semi-definiteness. Applies identically to each entry of imm_covariances (DatumShift.cpp:65-67) and to the MhtTracker per-node shift.
findings.failure_scenario: Turning target tracked by the IMM CT mode develops strong omega-vy correlation; ship transits 30 km, datum recenters. The 4x4 block rotates (currently also with the flipped sign, see the AxisRotation finding) while row/col 4 stays fixed; the resulting P is slightly inconsistent — in the worst case (correlation near 1) non-PSD, which downstream Cholesky/LLT-based likelihood evaluation rejects or turns into NaN d2.
findings.evidence: DatumShift.cpp:22-30 (only topLeftCorner<4,4> transformed; comment says 'smaller states skip' but says nothing about larger); ImmEstimator.cpp:448-467 (5-state means/covariances are the mainline carrier); CoordinatedTurn.cpp:12-51 (F couples omega into velocity, producing the cross terms).
findings.suggested_fix: In rotateCovarianceInPlace, build an n x n rotation (identity with R in the two 2x2 kinematic slots) sized to cov and apply the full similarity transform so cross terms to any additional states rotate too.

findings.title: onTrackConfirmed event carries the PREVIOUS observation's timestamp because Tracker calls recordHit before noteObservation
findings.description: recordHit fires onTrackConfirmed with last_observation_[i] as the event time (TrackManager.cpp:39), but all three Tracker call sites invoke recordHit BEFORE noteObservation(id, z.time) (Tracker.cpp:172-173, 272-273, 304-305). At the moment of confirmation, last_observation_ still holds the prior detection's timestamp, so the lifecycle event is stamped one full inter-detection interval early — the transition actually happened at z.time. TrackLifecycleEvent.time is documented as the snapshot 'on a track-lifecycle transition' (ITrackSink.hpp:9-13). onTrackUpdated for the same cycle then arrives with the later z.time, so a sink sees a Confirmed event that predates the update that caused it.
findings.failure_scenario: AIS class-B target reporting every 30 s, confirm_hits=3. The confirming (third) report arrives at t=60 s; the sink's onTrackConfirmed event says time=30 s. A consumer logging confirmation latency, ordering events by time, or replaying event streams sees the confirmation 30 s before the measurement that produced it (and before the onTrackUpdated event of the same cycle).
findings.evidence: TrackManager.cpp:30-42 (event uses last_observation_[i]); Tracker.cpp:172-174 (recordHit, then noteObservation, then recordUpdated with z.time); same ordering at 272-274 and 304-306.
findings.suggested_fix: Either have Tracker call noteObservation before recordHit, or pass the observation timestamp into recordHit and use it for the confirmation event.

findings.title: recordMiss fires onTrackDeleted before erasing while holding a live index; a sink that reenters the manager (recordMiss/add) corrupts the pending erase — wrong track deleted or unbounded recursion
findings.description: recordMiss computes index i, fires sink_->onTrackDeleted (line 51), and only afterwards erases tracks_/counters_/last_observation_ at i (lines 53-55). ITrackSink.hpp documents no reentrancy restriction ('Consumers (UI, logger, downstream pipelines) implement this'). If the sink calls back into the same TrackManager: (a) recordMiss on a lower-index track at its own delete threshold erases it, shifting the vectors, so the outer erase at the stale i removes the WRONG surviving track — that track disappears with no onTrackDeleted event while the id_to_index_ reindex loop masks the corruption; (b) recordMiss on the SAME id increments misses (still >= threshold) and fires the sink again — unbounded recursion, stack overflow. add() from the sink is survivable only by accident of ordering.
findings.failure_scenario: A consumer implements a 'cleanup companion tracks' sink: onTrackDeleted(e) calls mgr.recordMiss(partner_id) to retire a paired track. When the partner sits at a lower index and is already at delete_misses-1, the reentrant call erases it, and the outer frame then erases the element that shifted into position i — an unrelated healthy track is silently destroyed and its id vanishes from tracks() without any onTrackDeleted event.
findings.evidence: TrackManager.cpp:44-63 (sink call at :51 precedes erase at :53; index i captured at :45 and reused); ITrackSink.hpp:20-34 places no reentrancy constraint; no guard flag or deferred-event queue exists in the class.
findings.suggested_fix: Erase from the containers and fix the index map first, then fire onTrackDeleted from a local copy of the event; or set a reentrancy guard and document that sink callbacks must not call back into the manager.

units.coverage_notes:
SCOPE READ END-TO-END: core/tracking/{TrackManager,DatumShift,ClutterMapDetectionModel}.{hpp,cpp}, SensorDetectionModels.hpp; core/types/{Track,Measurement,Ids,Timestamp,MeasurementBuilders,SensorDefaults,StaticObstacle}.hpp(+.cpp). Cross-checked against core/geo/{AxisRotation,Datum}.cpp, core/projection/Projection.cpp, core/own_ship/OwnShipProvider, core/pipeline/Tracker.cpp (caller of TrackManager), core/collision/CpaEvaluator, ports/{ITrackSink,ISensorDetectionModel}.hpp, app examples, docs/output-contract.md, integration guide, and tests in tests/tracking + tests/types. The datum-rotation sign was verified by an independent Python replication of the exact Datum::toEnu math (finite-difference velocity through both frames) — not just by symbolic derivation. Checked the existing 10-bughunt-findings.md from this same review: no overlap (their pipeline batch explicitly lists DatumShift.cpp as out of scope; their TrackManager checks covered only erase+reindex mechanics, which I also verified correct).

VERIFIED CLEAN (hand-executed): TrackManager id allocation — single assignment site, monotonic uint64, caller-supplied ids overwritten, never reused after erase; id_to_index_ erase+reindex correct and pinned by test; determinism preserved (iteration driven by vector, unordered_map is lookup-only). DatumShift position path exact via geodetic round-trip (altitude carried through, curvature ~70 m z at 30 km handled correctly); omega itself correctly frame-invariant; rotateCovarianceInPlace .eval() prevents aliasing; particles/IMM means handled. projectRangeBearingToEnu Jacobian verified analytically (compass convention east=r*sin, J=[[sb,r*cb],[cb,-r*sb]]); heading sigma in quadrature per doc #16; isotropic GPS add is a documented approximation (ignores cross-scan correlation — standard). MeasurementBuilders: no-pose/no-datum empty-value sentinel honored by both in-repo callers (checked m.value.size()>0); bearing wrap irrelevant (sin/cos); heading composition max(pose sigma, floor) as documented; makeMeasurementFromEnuPosition isZero sentinel documented. ClutterMap EWMA math verified (seed=base*area so untouched-cell readback==baseline, w=1-exp(-dt/tau), first-touch prior weight, ratio clamp, bilinear cell centers consistent between cellOf/query/introspection, azimuth wrap incl. negative-index modulo); std::map keeps observe deterministic. CoverageSector::fromReturns traced for n=1, identical bearings, wrap-cut at largest gap, unwrap loop, largest-cluster tie-break — clean. FixedSensorDetectionModel lookup precedence and AdaptiveSensorDetectionModel EWMA/bbox traced. Timestamp: int64 nanos, exact comparisons, secondsSince diffs in integer domain first; no overflow before ~year 2262.

OVERFLOW ONE-LINERS (not reported): (1) Timestamp::fromSeconds loses sub-256 ns precision at Unix-epoch magnitudes (double ULP) and truncates toward zero — below sensor timing resolution. (2) TrackManager::add default first_observation=Timestamp{} (epoch 0) makes a direct-API track instantly stale in Tracker's sweep — pipeline always passes z.time. (3) ClutterMap EWMA gives w=0 for a same-timestamp second observe() on the same (kind,model) map — in-repo producers aggregate one bundle per key per scan so not hit. (4) ClutterMapParams.bearing_cell_rad=0 causes inf/UB size cast, no config validation (nonsensical config). (5) ClutterMap observe seeds new cells from the KIND-WIDE baseline while queries clamp around the SOURCE-keyed baseline — mixed but by-design kind-wide map. (6) TrackStatus::Deleted is never observable via TrackManager (immediate erase) though the output contract describes a final drain — PMBM-only semantics. (7) 'M-of-N' in the header is actually consecutive-counting (hit resets misses and vice versa) — alternating hit/miss never confirms; matches tests, likely intended. (8) SensorDefaults (ArpaTtm,RangeBearing2D)/(EoIr,RangeBearing2D) entries are unreachable via shipped adapters/builders (all emit Position2D) — dead table entries, related to my example.cpp finding.

OUT-OF-SCOPE OBSERVATIONS for other units: (a) core/output/TrackOutput.cpp:21 — toGeodeticWithCov rotates the ENU covariance but never applies the ENU->NED axis swap, while docs/output-contract.md:8 says row0=north/row1=east; for anisotropic covariance [0][0] is actually sigma_east^2 mislabeled as north (worked example is isotropic so no test catches it); it also inherits the AxisRotation sign flip — route to the output reviewer. (b) RangeBearing2D (range,bearing) pushed as ENU into ScanObservation.positions (MhtTracker.cpp:822, PmbmTracker.cpp:1797) — already reported by the pipeline batch. (c) Tracker::process/processBatch per-measurement miss ticking after timeout — already noted as pipeline overflow item. NOT COVERED: TrackTree.* (excluded from unit scope), estimator internals beyond initiate() layout, gitignored fixtures.

findings.title: datumAxisRotation does not wrap delta-longitude: datum recenter across the antimeridian rotates every track velocity/covariance by a wildly wrong convergence angle
findings.description: datumAxisRotation computes delta_lon_rad = (n.lon_deg - o.lon_deg) * kDeg2Rad with no normalization to [-180, 180). Longitudes arriving from adapters are in [-180, 180], so when the two datum origins straddle the antimeridian the raw difference is ~±359.7 deg instead of ~±0.3 deg. gamma = delta_lon * sin(mean_lat) then becomes ~-6.28*sin(lat) rad instead of ~+0.003 rad. This feeds (a) shiftStateOnDatumChange / shiftTracksOnDatumChange (core/tracking/DatumShift.cpp:39,73), which rotate every track's velocity, covariance blocks, IMM means/covs and particle velocities on each auto-recenter, and (b) toGeodeticWithCov (core/output/TrackOutput.cpp:17), which rotates the output position covariance into the target's NED frame for targets across the antimeridian from the datum. Positions themselves are shifted exactly via the geodetic round-trip, so the corruption is silent: positions stay right while velocities/covariances are rotated wrong.
findings.failure_scenario: Own-ship transits east across the antimeridian at lat 30 N (mid-Pacific; same for Bering Strait at 60 N). Working datum origin lon = +179.90. Ship sails ~31 km east; the next fix at lon = -179.78 exceeds the 30 km threshold and OwnShipProvider auto-recenters. datumAxisRotation(old=+179.90, new=-179.78) computes delta_lon = -359.68 deg, gamma = -6.277 * sin(30) = -3.139 rad (should be +0.0029 rad). R(-3.139) is approximately -I: shiftTracksOnDatumChange reverses the velocity vector of EVERY live track (a 10 kn northbound target becomes 10 kn southbound). All subsequent predictions run backwards, gating fails, tracks fragment or drag. At lat 45 the rotation error is ~105 deg (mod 2pi), corrupting velocity direction and covariance orientation instead of reversing. Independently, toGeodeticWithCov reports output covariance ellipses rotated by ~2*pi*(1-sin(lat)) for any target across the meridian even without a recenter. No test covers longitudes near +/-180 (tests/geo/test_datum.cpp, tests/tracking/test_datum_shift.cpp all use lon 0-8.5; the convergence-angle test uses lon 0->1), so the suite cannot contradict this.
findings.evidence: AxisRotation.cpp lines 15-17: `const double delta_lon_rad = (n.lon_deg - o.lon_deg) * kDeg2Rad; ... const double gamma = delta_lon_rad * std::sin(mean_lat_rad);` — no fmod/wrap anywhere. Call sites confirmed at core/tracking/DatumShift.cpp:39,73 and core/output/TrackOutput.cpp:17; MhtTracker::onDatumRecentered (core/pipeline/MhtTracker.cpp:846) routes through the same shiftStateOnDatumChange. OwnShipProvider.cpp:39-44 shows the recenter fires whenever horizontal ENU distance > 30 km, with the new origin taken verbatim from pose.lon_deg.
findings.suggested_fix: Normalize the longitude difference before scaling: wrap (n.lon_deg - o.lon_deg) into [-180, 180) (e.g. d = std::remainder(n.lon_deg - o.lon_deg, 360.0)) in datumAxisRotation, and add an antimeridian regression test for both DatumShift and toGeodeticWithCov.

verdict.reasoning: Independently traced the full failing path. (1) core/geo/AxisRotation.cpp:15 computes the raw longitude difference with no wrapping; grep confirms no fmod/wrap/normalize anywhere in core/geo/, DatumShift.cpp, or TrackOutput.cpp. (2) Longitudes are provably bounded to [-180,180]: ecefToGeodetic (Wgs84.cpp:37) uses atan2, and Datum stores its origin verbatim, so straddling the antimeridian yields a raw delta of ~±359.7 deg. (3) OwnShipProvider.cpp:33-45 auto-recenters by default (enable_auto_recenter{true}, 30 km threshold, OwnShipProvider.hpp:74-75) using pose.lon_deg verbatim. Hand-executed the scenario: old datum lon +179.90, new pose lon -179.78 at lat 30 N is ~30.9 km ENU (recenter fires); gamma = -359.68 deg * sin(30) = -179.84 deg instead of the correct +0.16 deg, so R is approximately -I. (4) DatumShift.cpp confirms the silent-corruption split exactly as claimed: positions shift via the exact geodetic round-trip (lines 6-12, correct since ECEF is periodic in lon) while velocity (44), the 4x4 covariance block (46), IMM means (57-62), IMM covariances (65-67), and particle velocities (85-90) all get the bad rotation — every live track's velocity is reversed. (5) The independent output path is also real: TrackOutput.cpp:13-17 builds target_datum from ecefToGeodetic output, so a target ~30 km across the antimeridian gets its output NED covariance rotated by the same wrong gamma with no recenter needed. (6) No guard or test exists: tests/tracking/test_datum_shift.cpp uses lon 0-8.5, tests/geo/ has nothing near ±180, and neither AxisRotation.hpp's doc comment nor docs/algorithms mentions an antimeridian limitation. Not covered by the known/by-design list.
verdict.corrected_details: Confirmed exactly as claimed. One refinement: the failure requires either (a) an auto-recenter whose old/new datum origins straddle the antimeridian (auto-recenter is on by default at 30 km, so any transit through Bering Strait, mid-Pacific, or NZ/Fiji waters triggers it), or (b) toGeodeticWithCov for a target across the antimeridian from the datum (corrupts only the reported covariance orientation, not state). Path (a) is the severe one: R(gamma) with gamma off by delta_lon*sin(lat) mod 2pi reverses every live track's velocity at lat 30 (error ~180 deg) and rotates it ~105 deg at lat 45, plus corrupts covariance blocks, IMM means/covs, and particle velocities, while positions remain exactly right — so the corruption is silent until predictions run backwards and gating fails. Fix is a one-line wrap of delta_lon into [-180,180) (e.g. std::remainder(delta_lon_deg, 360.0)) before computing gamma. High, not critical: geographically confined to antimeridian operation and positions stay correct, but it is silent whole-track-table corruption in a collision-avoidance-relevant system with zero test coverage near ±180.

findings.title: Maneuver gate is self-defeating: its noise envelope is computed from maneuver-contaminated residuals, so the effective suppression threshold is ~8.8x the configured one and biased own-ship velocity is published during moderate maneuvers
findings.description: The two-halves maneuver gate compares dv against gate = maneuver_dv_threshold_mps + 3*sqrt(2)*sigma_v_half, where sigma_v_half = sigma_pos/sqrt(sxx_half) and sigma_pos is derived from the FULL-window constant-velocity fit residuals (lines 77-79). During a genuine maneuver those residuals contain the maneuver signal itself, so the envelope grows linearly with the very dv the gate is supposed to detect. Hand-execution with zero GPS noise, 1 Hz, window 8 (defaults), velocity step dv at the half boundary (best case for detection): ss_res = 2.619*dv^2, sigma_pos = 0.467*dv, envelope = 0.886*dv, so the gate trips only when dv > 0.5 + 0.886*dv, i.e. dv > 4.4 m/s — 8.8x the configured 0.5 m/s threshold (the coefficient is the same for any axis split; off-boundary maneuvers are detected even less). For any 0.5 < dv < 4.4 the estimator publishes the full-window blended slope (error ~0.405*dv vs the current true velocity) with sigma_v = sigma_pos/sqrt(42) ~ 0.072*dv — about 5.6 sigma overconfident. The design doc (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-03-adaptive-uere-design.md §4.4) explicitly states the gate must 'suppress publication' during maneuvers and claims 'no chicken-and-egg'; the chicken-and-egg is real. UereEstimator.cpp:112 shares the identical formula, though its failure direction there is only modest sigma inflation.
findings.failure_scenario: GGA-only nav feed (no RMC; enable_velocity_estimator defaults true and is the documented fallback, OwnShipNmeaAdapter.cpp:180-184). Own ship at 5 m/s makes a sustained 3 deg/s turn: over the 8 s window the course changes ~24 deg, dv = 2*5*sin(12 deg) ~ 2.1 m/s — 4x the configured gate threshold, yet gate = 0.5 + 0.886*2.1 = 2.36 > 2.1, so publication is NOT suppressed. pose.velocity_enu is published ~0.85 m/s wrong with sigma_v ~ 0.15 m/s (floored at 0.05), velocity_is_valid = true, for the whole duration of the turn. This own-ship velocity feeds CpaOwnShip (core/collision/CpaOwnShip.cpp:15) and hence collision-risk CPA/TCPA — wrong precisely during own-ship maneuvers, when CPA changes matter most. The existing test SuppressesDuringManeuver (tests/own_ship/test_own_ship_velocity_estimator.cpp:40) uses dv = 7.07 m/s, which clears the inflated gate (7.07 > 0.5 + 0.886*7.07 = 6.76) by only 4%, so the suite pins only the extreme case and does not contradict this finding.
findings.evidence: Lines 77-79: `var = (fit_x.ss_res + fit_y.ss_res)/(2*(n-2)); sigma_pos = sqrt(var)` — full-window residuals include the maneuver. Lines 123-127: `sigma_v_half = sigma_pos/sqrt(sxx_half); noise_envelope = 3*sqrt(2)*sigma_v_half; gate = cfg_.maneuver_dv_threshold_mps + noise_envelope`. With defaults (1 Hz, n=8): sxx_half = 5, sxx_full = 42; worked numbers above verified by direct computation of the LS fit on x = [0,0,0,0,dv,2dv,3dv,4dv].
findings.suggested_fix: Estimate sigma_pos for the envelope from maneuver-robust residuals — e.g. the pooled residuals of the two per-half fits (each half is approximately constant-velocity even mid-maneuver) instead of the full-window fit — or cap the envelope by an a-priori GPS noise bound. Add a test that a dv of 2-3x the threshold with zero noise is suppressed.

findings.title: approxMeters does not wrap delta-longitude: crossing the antimeridian raises a false position_jump nav-health flag
findings.description: approxMeters computes deast = (lon2 - lon1) * 111320 * cos(mean_lat) with no wrap. For consecutive fixes straddling +/-180 (prev lon +179.999, curr lon -179.999) the raw difference is ~-360 deg, giving a position step of ~2.2e7 m. evaluateNavInput then sets position_jump (step/dt >> 50 m/s) and reports position_step_m ~ 2e7; the derived SOG also becomes huge, which additionally suppresses a legitimate heading_unreliable_low_sog flag for that pair. The guard never rewrites poses, so the damage is confined to one spurious 'degrade visibly' health event per crossing (subsequent pose pairs are on the same side and evaluate correctly).
findings.failure_scenario: Vessel crosses the antimeridian westbound at 5 m/s with a 1 Hz GPS feed and a nav-health sink wired (setNavHealthSink). At the crossing pair, onNavHealth fires with position_jump=true and position_step_m ~ 2.2e7 m even though the true step is ~5 m. An operator display or automated degradation logic wired to INavHealthSink reports a GPS glitch that never happened.
findings.evidence: NavInputGuard.cpp lines 14-19: `const double deast = (lon2 - lon1) * 111320.0 * std::cos(mean_lat);` — no remainder/wrap, unlike headingDeltaDeg (line 22) which does wrap. tests/own_ship/test_nav_input_guard.cpp exercises only lon 8.0 +/- small deltas, so nothing pins antimeridian behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the longitude difference: `double dlon = std::remainder(lon2 - lon1, 360.0);` before scaling.

findings.title: Auto-recenter acts on a pose the nav guard just flagged as a position jump: a single >30 km GPS glitch recenters the datum twice and churns all datum sinks
findings.description: update() evaluates the nav guard (which can set position_jump for a physically implausible step) but ignores the result for its own state transitions: the recenter check at lines 35-45 runs unconditionally on the incoming pose. A single glitched fix far from the current origin (>30 km) immediately re-anchors the working datum onto the bogus position and fires every IDatumChangeSink; the next good fix is then >30 km from the glitch origin and recenters again. Each recenter shifts all tracks (numerically near-lossless), but also clears MhtTracker::contribution_history_ (MhtTracker.cpp:846-864, bias-extraction gap by design 'a recenter is rare') and re-anchors the LiveOccupancyModel grid, which is lossy for occupancy content — two spurious re-anchors wipe the suppression map. The provider knows the pose is implausible (its own guard just measured step/dt > max_position_speed_mps) yet re-anchors on it anyway; the guard's flag-only contract covers not rewriting the pose, but recentering is the provider's own state, not the pose.
findings.failure_scenario: 1 Hz GPS feed; receiver glitch (cold-start jump / multipath / spoofing) emits one fix 100 km off, then resumes normal fixes. t=k: guard fires position_jump AND the datum recenters onto the glitch (all sinks fire, occupancy grid re-anchors 100 km away, dropping its contents; MHT bias history cleared). t=k+1: good fix is 100 km from the glitch origin — second recenter, second full sink churn, occupancy content lost again. Net effect from one bad sentence: occupancy/suppression state destroyed and every track shifted twice, silently.
findings.evidence: OwnShipProvider.cpp lines 28-31 (guard evaluated, result only forwarded to the sink) and 35-45 (recenter decision uses only the ENU distance of the raw pose; no plausibility condition, no consultation of the NavHealth result computed four lines earlier). CLAUDE.md invariant 6 places plausibility validation in adapters, but backlog #18 added the guard here precisely because implausible fixes reach this layer.
findings.suggested_fix: When the guard is wired and reports position_jump for the incoming pose, skip (or defer) the recenter for that pose — e.g. require two consecutive far fixes before re-anchoring.

units.coverage_notes: Read end-to-end: core/geo/Wgs84.{hpp,cpp}, Datum.{hpp,cpp}, AxisRotation.{hpp,cpp}; core/projection/Projection.{hpp,cpp}; core/own_ship/OwnShipProvider.{hpp,cpp}, NavInputGuard.{hpp,cpp}, OwnShipVelocityEstimator.{hpp,cpp}, UereEstimator.{hpp,cpp}; plus call-site files core/tracking/DatumShift.cpp, core/output/TrackOutput.cpp, core/pipeline/MhtTracker.cpp (recenter path), core/types/MeasurementBuilders.cpp, core/collision/CpaOwnShip.cpp, adapters/own_ship/OwnShipNmeaAdapter (estimator feed), and tests (test_wgs84, test_datum, test_datum_shift, test_nav_input_guard, test_own_ship_velocity_estimator, test_uere_estimator). CLEAN: WGS84 constants and Bowring inverse verified against standard formulas (round-trip test passes at 1e-9 deg); only exact-pole input (p=0) yields a wrong altitude — unrealistic for maritime, not reported. Datum ECEF->ENU rotation matrix verified row-by-row; toEnu/toGeodetic are exact 3D transforms (no small-angle error), so the 30 km recenter bound is consistent — no accuracy-vs-distance defect in the transform itself; DatumShift's z=0 round-trip is exact for the 3D point (no horizontal corruption from the ~70 m curvature drop). Projection.cpp polar->Cartesian Jacobian and covariance composition (bearing+heading variance in quadrature, isotropic GPS term) verified correct and PSD by construction. OwnShipProvider: sorted insert / equal-timestamp (most-recent-wins) semantics verified consistent with poseAtOrBefore's reverse walk; measurement-before-first-pose handled gracefully in MeasurementBuilders (empty measurement returned; datum() throw documented and guarded by hasDatum()); recenter fires sinks after the datum swap but before inserting the triggering pose — checked every IDatumChangeSink implementation in-tree (TrackShifter, CoastlineModel, StaticObstacleModel, BearingWedgeModel, LiveOccupancyModel, MhtTracker, FoxgloveDebugRecorder): none reads provider state during the callback, so no half-updated-state observer exists today (latent trap only). NavInputGuard: heading delta wrap correct; dt<=0 / out-of-order / first-pose paths correct and test-pinned; units of all thresholds consistent. LS fit math (fitAxis) in both estimators verified; degenerate windows (sxx=0, window<4, n<=2) return unpublished; Timestamp nanos->double precision adequate (~us). Overflow one-liners, mostly out of scope but relevant to other reviewers: (1) adapters/own_ship/OwnShipNmeaAdapter.cpp:139 equirectangular reference is fixed at the FIRST GGA fix forever — cos(ref_lat) east-scale error grows on long voyages (~2x from 10N to 60N, biasing the published fallback velocity) and a +/-180 lon crossing injects a 2.2e7 m step into both estimators (transient garbage sigma/velocity published for ~1 window); (2) a pure position step (GPS fix-mode shift) aligned with the two-halves boundary evades the maneuver gate entirely (dv~0) and publishes a phantom velocity ~0.19*step m/s — same root family as finding 2; (3) NavInputGuard's derived-SOG-from-step is noise-dominated at high fix rates (anchored ship, 10 Hz, 1 m noise => derived SOG ~10 m/s => documented low-sog anchor case never flags) — accuracy limit of the documented fact-free design, not reported as a defect; (4) both estimators split halves by arrival order, not time order — only an issue if fed out-of-order, which the NMEA adapter does not do.

findings.title: Closed-loop heading-bias wiring double-subtracts the published bias: estimate converges to half the true gyro bias
findings.description: observe(AisArpaPairObservation) treats z = wrap(beta_arpa - beta_ais) as a measurement of the FULL bias b and forms the residual y = wrap(z - b_hat_). But in the documented wiring (integration-guide.md §5: pass the estimator to ArpaAdapter/EoIrAdapter 'so the estimated bias is removed from their bearings'; same wiring in tests/sim/BusComparisonHelpers.hpp runBusCellInterleaved and test_bus_anchor_loss.cpp), ArpaAdapter.cpp:119 already subtracts the published b_hat from the TTM bearing before projecting to ENU, so the SourceTouch the extractor pairs is ALREADY debiased and z measures only the residual (b_true - b_pub). Subtracting b_hat again gives y = b_true - 2*b_hat once is_published flips true. The KF fixed point of b_hat += k*(b_true - 2*b_hat) is b_hat = b_true/2: the published estimate decays from the correct value to half, and every subsequent ARPA/EO-IR measurement permanently retains half the heading bias while the published variance keeps shrinking below the (0.3 deg)^2 publish threshold (overconfident). Correct closed-loop math with pre-corrected inputs is y = z (or un-correct the touch); y = z - b_hat is only right open-loop.
findings.failure_scenario: Guide-§5 wiring, true gyro bias 2 deg, AIS+ARPA target at 1.5 km. Pre-publish the estimator correctly converges to ~2 deg and publishes; the adapter starts subtracting 2 deg; the next pairs give z ~ 0 deg, y = z - b_hat ~ -2 deg (well inside the 5-sigma gate since sigma_v ~ 1.1 deg), dragging b_hat down with time constant of a few pairs to the 1 deg equilibrium. Result: a permanent ~1 deg residual bias (~26 m cross-range at 1.5 km) on every ARPA/EO-IR measurement, with published sigma claiming <0.3 deg. For b_true > ~5.5 deg the post-publish y instead fails the outlier gate: all pairs rejected, last_update freezes, estimator unpublishes 30 s later, correction switches off, raw pairs get re-accepted, it re-publishes — a ~30 s on/off oscillation of the entire correction. No test contradicts: tests/bias unit tests are open-loop (observations built from raw positions), test_bus_anchor_loss.cpp injects bias_deg = 0.0, and test_bus_bias_estimator_sweep.cpp is SUCCEED-only (OSPA printed, never asserts converged bias; half-correction still improves OSPA vs none).
findings.evidence: core/bias/HeadingBiasEstimator.cpp:46,64 (z and y = z - b_hat); adapters/arpa/ArpaAdapter.cpp:112-119 and adapters/eoir/EoIrAdapter.cpp:38-45 (bearing_corrected = bearing - b_hat when is_published); core/bias/AisArpaPairExtractor.cpp:49-50 (pairs the corrected touch values verbatim); docs/integration-guide.md §5 ('pass it to the sensor adapters ... so the estimated bias is removed from their bearings' alongside 'extract AIS↔ARPA pairs ... feed them to observe'); tests/sim/BusComparisonHelpers.hpp:490-493 documents this exact loop as the intended closed loop.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one convention: either have the extractor un-correct the touch (store/carry the raw bearing, or add back the bias the adapter applied), or drop the '- b_hat_' when the observation comes from already-corrected data (y = z). Cleanest: carry the applied correction in the Measurement/SourceTouch so the estimator can reconstruct the full-bias measurement, and add a closed-loop test asserting b_hat converges to the injected bias (not half) after publish.

findings.title: Per-sensor bias loop feeds bias-corrected SourceTouches back into an estimator that subtracts b_hat again: published mounting bias converges to half
findings.description: observe(PositionBiasPairObservation) forms r = z_sensor - z_anchor - b_hat (line 99), i.e. it models z_sensor as the RAW biased measurement. But in the shipped item-9 wiring (core/benchmark/Sweep.cpp:318-336 and 500-520; PMBM path identical), the tracker is given the same estimator via setSensorBiasProvider, applyBiasCorrection subtracts the published bias from every incoming measurement (Tracker.cpp:136, MhtTracker.cpp:242-249, PmbmTracker.cpp:1354), and the SourceTouch is populated from the CORRECTED measurement (Tracker.cpp:156-160, MhtTracker.cpp:619+642-649). So once positionBias(key).is_published flips true, the extractor's z_sensor already excludes b_pub and the observation becomes r = (b_true - b_pub) - b_hat = b_true - 2*b_hat; the fixed point is b_hat = b_true/2. Identical defect on the bearing path: applyBiasCorrection subtracts the published bearing bias from Bearing2D value(0) (BiasCorrection.hpp:72), the touch stores the corrected alpha_rad (SourceTouchPopulate.hpp:69), and observe(BearingBiasPairObservation) adds b_hat into alpha_pred (SensorBiasEstimator.cpp:142) — a second subtraction in the residual.
findings.failure_scenario: Sweep item-9 wiring (also the guide's 'wire it only if you have mounting misalignment' path): radar with a true 4 m east offset. Estimator converges to ~4 m, publishes when P <= 1 m^2 per axis with an anchored observation; applyBiasCorrection starts shifting radar measurements; next cycle's pair gives r ~ -4 m (0.3 sigma of S ~ P + R_obs with the 10 m fallbacks, so the Mahalanobis gate accepts), and b_hat decays to the 2 m equilibrium. Every corrected radar position permanently retains a 2 m offset while the published covariance claims <= 1 m per axis; there is no staleness gate on positionBias, so this never self-heals. For offsets > ~5 sigma of S the post-publish r is instead rejected forever (rej_outlier climbs, estimate frozen, drift untrackable). Unit tests (PositionConvergesToTrueBias etc.) feed raw synthetic observations open-loop, so they pass; no closed-loop test asserts the converged value through a tracker.
findings.evidence: core/bias/SensorBiasEstimator.cpp:98-99 (r = z_sensor - z_anchor - b_hat) and 142-143 (alpha_pred = atan2 + b_hat); core/pipeline/Tracker.cpp:136 + 156-160; core/pipeline/MhtTracker.cpp:242-249 + 619 + 642-649 (touches built from the corrected scan); core/pipeline/BiasCorrection.hpp:60-86; core/benchmark/Sweep.cpp:318 (setSensorBiasProvider(bias_est.get())) + 321-336 (same instance fed by extractPositionPairs/extractBearingPairs post-scan).
findings.suggested_fix: Same convention decision as the heading finding: either populate SourceTouches from the PRE-correction measurement (keep the correction filter-internal), or carry the applied bias in the touch so the extractor can reconstruct the raw pair, or drop the '- b_hat' / '+ b_hat' terms when inputs are pre-corrected. Add a closed-loop test (tracker + provider + extractor) asserting the published bias equals the injected offset at steady state.

findings.title: v1 heading-bias pairs compute bearings about the datum origin, not own-ship: NMEA ArpaAdapter never populates sensor_position_enu
findings.description: extractPairs sets obs.own_position_enu = arpa->sensor_position_enu (line 48), and HeadingBiasEstimator::observe computes both bearings relative to that point (HeadingBiasEstimator.cpp:41-44). But the production NMEA ArpaAdapter never sets Measurement::sensor_position_enu on either TTM (ArpaAdapter.cpp:140-148 — own_xy is computed at line 110 and used for projection but never copied into m) or TLL (lines 77-86) measurements, violating Measurement.hpp:67-71's documented contract ('for a sensor mounted on a moving platform this is the platform's ENU position'); the field stays at its Vector2d::Zero() default, flows into the SourceTouch (Tracker.cpp:157, MhtTracker.cpp:647), and the estimator ends up measuring the angle SUBTENDED AT THE DATUM ORIGIN between the AIS and ARPA target positions instead of the bearing disagreement at own-ship. The bias-induced AIS-ARPA displacement is b*r_ownship-to-target; seen from the origin it subtends ~b*(r_own-to-tgt/r_origin-to-tgt), rotated by the geometry difference. Additionally r_ais (used for the <1 m guard and for sigma_v2 = (sigma_ais/r_ais)^2) and r_arpa (GPS floor) become distances from the origin, so the assumed observation noise is understated by the same ratio — the estimator converges CONFIDENTLY to a wrong value. The replay adapters (PlotCsvReplayAdapter.cpp:116/176, AutoferryJsonReplay.cpp:276/296) and MeasurementBuilders.cpp:82 all set the field, confirming the ArpaAdapter omission. extractPositionPairs' min-range gate (own_position_enu = t.sensor_position_enu, SensorBiasPairExtractor.cpp:72 + SensorBiasEstimator.cpp:89-95) is likewise measured from the origin.
findings.failure_scenario: OwnShipProvider auto-datum initializes at the first fix; own-ship transits 20 km (below the 30 km recenter). AIS-equipped target 2 km from own-ship, true gyro bias 2 deg: the 70 m AIS-ARPA displacement subtends only ~0.2 deg at the origin (10x dilution, direction rotated), while sigma_v2 is computed with r_ais ~ 20 km — 10x understated AIS noise. The estimator converges quickly and publishes a bias that is a geometry-dependent fraction of the truth (possibly wrong-signed in component), which the adapters then subtract from every ARPA/EO-IR bearing. Every in-repo sim places own-ship at or within a few hundred meters of the datum origin (test_bus_anchor_loss.cpp: own-ship stationary at the origin), so no test sees it.
findings.evidence: core/bias/AisArpaPairExtractor.cpp:48; core/bias/HeadingBiasEstimator.cpp:41-62 (ais_rel/arpa_rel/r_ais/r_arpa all relative to obs.own_position_enu); adapters/arpa/ArpaAdapter.cpp:109-110 (own_xy computed) vs 140-148 (m.sensor_position_std_m set, m.sensor_position_enu not set) and 77-86 (TLL never consults own pose); core/types/Measurement.hpp:67-71 (contract); core/types/Track.hpp:88 (zero default).
findings.suggested_fix: Set m.sensor_position_enu = own_xy in ArpaAdapter's TTM branch (and resolve own pose for TLL); defensively, extractPairs could refuse pairs whose ARPA touch carries a zero sensor position while own_position_std_m > 0, or the estimator could gate on r_ais vs r_arpa consistency. Add a sim test with own-ship displaced several km from the datum.

findings.title: Cross-sensor Schmidt fold debiases the anchor touch a second time when the tracker already applied the published bias
findings.description: extractCrossSensorPositionPairs sets obs.z_anchor_enu = best_y->value_enu - best_b_anchor (line 259), where best_b_anchor is Y's published bias — designed for RAW touches. In the shipped wiring (Sweep.cpp:318 + 334-336: the same SensorBiasEstimator is both the tracker's setSensorBiasProvider and the extractor's bias_provider), Y's touch value was ALREADY debiased by applyBiasCorrection before the touch was recorded (MhtTracker.cpp:242-249+646, Tracker.cpp:136+156), so the anchor is debiased twice: z_anchor ~ truth + b_y_true - 2*b_pub_y ~ truth - b_y once Y has converged. Every observation for the calibrated key X then carries a systematic error equal to Y's bias, and because is_anchor_source = best_anchor_published = true for exactly these pairs, the corrupted X estimate is allowed to publish and shift all of X's measurements. The test CrossSensorAppliesSchmidtFoldFromProvider builds raw touches by hand, so it validates the extractor in isolation and does not exercise the tracker-corrected-touch composition.
findings.failure_scenario: Item-13 wiring with two radars and a lidar (no AIS in window): lidar Y has a 3 m east offset, seeded/refined and published; tracker corrects Y's measurements, touches store corrected values; line 259 subtracts the 3 m again, making the anchor read truth - 3 m east. Radar X, perfectly aligned, receives pair residuals r ~ +3 m every cycle (plus its own double-subtract from the sibling finding) and converges to a phantom ~3 m bias flagged anchor-published — X's measurements are then shifted 3 m off truth by applyBiasCorrection.
findings.evidence: core/bias/SensorBiasPairExtractor.cpp:230-259 (fold and z_anchor); core/benchmark/Sweep.cpp:318,334-336 (same estimator as provider and observer); core/pipeline/MhtTracker.cpp:242-249,642-649 (touches from corrected scan); tests/bias/test_sensor_bias_estimator.cpp:559 (fold test uses hand-built raw touches).
findings.suggested_fix: Resolve together with the raw-vs-corrected touch convention (previous finding): if touches become raw, line 259 is correct as-is; if touches stay corrected, the fold must NOT subtract the published bias again (and R_anchor should still add P_b_Y). Add a two-sensor closed-loop test with one seeded-published sensor asserting the unbiased sensor stays at zero.

findings.title: v2 bearing-innovation outlier gate is centered on zero, not on b_hat: a true bias above ~5x bearing sigma starves the observer before it can publish
findings.description: observe(BearingInnovation) gates on |obs.innovation_rad| > bi_outlier_sigma * sqrt(S + p_b) — the RAW innovation against zero — while the update residual is y = wrap(r - b_hat) (line 109). Since bearing measurements are never heading-corrected on this path, genuine innovations stay r ~ b_true forever. As p_b shrinks from the accepted updates, the acceptance threshold 5*sqrt(S + p_b) tightens toward 5*sqrt(S); for any true bias b_true > ~5*sqrt(S) the gate permanently closes at p_b ~ (b_true/5sigma)^2 - S, which for realistic S is far ABOVE the (0.3 deg)^2 publish threshold — so the estimator absorbs most of the bias, then rejects every subsequent observation, never publishes (or goes stale and unpublishes within stale_seconds), and the correction never engages despite abundant clean data. This matches the spec formula (2026-06-04-multi-track-bearing-bias-observer-design.md §4 G3), but it is exactly the failure the v1 path's gate was reworked to avoid (HeadingBiasEstimator.cpp:66-73 review #14 comment: gating against a reference that excludes the estimate 'would reject any genuinely large uncalibrated bias forever'); v1 and applyScalarUpdate both gate on the residual |y|, v2 alone gates on |r|.
findings.failure_scenario: v2-only wiring (Tracker::setBearingInnovationSink, AIS-anchored track feeding a raw-bearing camera), uncalibrated installation with 8 deg gyro bias, bearing sigma 1 deg: cold start accepts (threshold 5*sqrt(1+25 deg^2) ~ 25 deg); after enough updates p_b crosses ~1.6 deg^2 and the threshold drops below 8 deg — every further innovation rejected (rej_outlier climbs), last_update freezes, p_b (1.6 deg^2) never reaches the 0.09 deg^2 publish threshold, and with process noise 9.4e-11 rad^2/s the gate stays closed for days. The estimator holds a good ~7.5 deg internal estimate that is never published and never applied. Unit test OutlierGateRejectsHugeInnovation pins single-shot rejection only; no test runs v2 to convergence with a large bias.
findings.evidence: core/bias/HeadingBiasEstimator.cpp:102-115 (gate on obs.innovation_rad, update on y = r - b_hat); contrast lines 74-78 (v1 gates on |y|, cold-start exempt) and 141-145 (applyScalarUpdate gates on |y|); docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-04-multi-track-bearing-bias-observer-design.md:84 (G3 as coded).
findings.suggested_fix: Gate v2 on the residual |wrap(r - b_hat)| <= N*sqrt(S + p_b) (consistent with v1 and the mhs kinds), or keep the zero-centered gate but only until first acceptance (mirror v1's cold-start exemption logic inverted). Update the spec's G3 row with the rationale.

findings.title: applyBiasCorrection silently skips PositionVelocity2D, so a published position bias is applied to only part of a source's stream
findings.description: The model dispatch handles Position2D, Bearing2D, and RangeBearing2D only. MeasurementModel::PositionVelocity2D is a first-class model (SourceTouchPopulate.hpp:37-44 handles it; remote-track/velocity-carrying feeds emit it), and its value(0..1) is the same ENU position the Position2D branch shifts — but a published position bias for the same SensorBiasKey is never applied to it and its covariance is never inflated by P_b. A source that mixes the two models (velocity present on some reports only) gets a stream whose position alternates by the full bias magnitude. NOTE: overlaps a LOW finding already recorded in docs/reviews/2026-07-09-prerelease-review (10-bughunt-findings.md:228); re-verified here — the dispatch omission is real.
findings.failure_scenario: A shore radar feed with a published 40 m registration bias emits PositionVelocity2D when its tracker reports velocity and Position2D otherwise: corrected reports land on truth, uncorrected ones 40 m off, producing 40 m innovation zig-zag on the same track and biased velocity estimates — the opposite of what the published sub-1-m bias covariance claims.
findings.evidence: core/pipeline/BiasCorrection.hpp:60-86 (no PositionVelocity2D branch); core/pipeline/SourceTouchPopulate.hpp:37-44 (model exists on this pipeline path); prior-round finding 10-bughunt-findings.md:228.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a PositionVelocity2D branch mirroring Position2D (shift value(0..1), add P_b to covariance block<2,2>(0,0)); decide and document that the velocity components carry no bias term.

findings.title: No R>0 validation / variance floor in the scalar updates: a zero-sigma observation collapses p_b to exactly 0 and locks the filter onto one noisy sample
findings.description: applyScalarUpdate (and the v1 path via sigma_v2) accepts R = 0: s = p_b, k = 1, b_hat snaps exactly to the measurement and p_b becomes exactly 0. All three v3 observation structs default their sigma fields to 0.0 (HeadingBiasObservations.hpp:20,36,53), so a consumer constructing GyroVsGpsHeadingObservation directly (the guide's 'wire any subset' contract) and forgetting the std gets this by default. After the collapse, sigma = sqrt(q*dt) is ~2e-5 rad per second of elapsed time, so every subsequent genuine observation (noise ~0.5 deg) fails the 5-sigma outlier gate; last_update freezes, the estimator publishes a single-sample bias for stale_seconds and then unpublishes, permanently locked (p_b regrows at 9.4e-11 rad^2/s — ~days to reopen the gate). The in-repo OwnShipNmeaAdapter populates the sigmas from config, so only direct constructors hit it — but these structs are the documented consumer surface and no entry point validates R.
findings.failure_scenario: Consumer feeds gyro-vs-GPS-heading observations at 1 Hz without setting gps_true_heading_std_rad: at t=0 the estimator publishes bias = (first sample's noise + bias) with variance 0; for the next 30 s adapters subtract that noisy value; at t=30 it unpublishes; rej_mhs_outlier climbs by one per second forever; no error is ever surfaced.
findings.evidence: core/bias/HeadingBiasEstimator.cpp:135-150 (no R>0 check; k = p_b/s with s = R + p_b); core/bias/HeadingBiasObservations.hpp:20,36,53 (0.0 sigma defaults); adapters/own_ship/OwnShipNmeaAdapter.cpp:235,260,317 (adapter path sets sigmas from config).
findings.suggested_fix: Reject (or floor) observations with non-positive R at each observe() entry point, and/or give the sigma fields non-zero defaults; optionally add a small floor to p_b after update (the estimator currently relies solely on process noise).

findings.title: Staleness un-publish never triggers unless the consumer keeps calling predictTo: 'now' is last_predict_, which freezes on anchor loss
findings.description: current() computes age_s = deltaSeconds(last_predict_, last_update_). last_predict_ only advances inside observe(...) or an explicit predictTo() call. In a v1-only wiring (guide §5 shows extractPairs -> observe and never mentions predictTo), an AIS dropout stops all observe() calls, so last_predict_ freezes together with last_update_, age stays ~0, and is_published remains true indefinitely with an aging bias — the exact scenario stale_seconds (30 s default) exists to catch. The in-repo closed-loop tests pass only because they explicitly call estimator.predictTo(tick) every truth tick (test_bus_anchor_loss.cpp:200, BusComparisonHelpers.hpp:558); a consumer following the guide gets no such cadence. Wirings that include v3 own-ship observations are immune (each own-ship fix calls predictTo internally), which is why I rate this medium confidence / low severity: it requires a v1-only (or v1+v2-only) wiring plus total source loss.
findings.failure_scenario: Consumer wires only the AIS<->ARPA pair path per guide §5. AIS transponder traffic ceases at t=60 s (dropout, jamming, open sea); no observe() calls follow; at t=600 s current().is_published is still true and adapters keep applying the last bias with its frozen 'converged' variance, despite the documented 30 s staleness contract (test_bus_anchor_loss asserts unpublish by t=90 s, but only with external predictTo ticks).
findings.evidence: core/bias/HeadingBiasEstimator.cpp:118-130 (age from last_predict_); tests/sim/test_bus_anchor_loss.cpp:200 (explicit per-tick predictTo keeps the contract alive); docs/integration-guide.md §5 (wiring shown without any periodic predictTo).
findings.suggested_fix: Either document predictTo(now) as a REQUIRED periodic call for the staleness contract (guide + header), or let adapters/tracker drive time into the estimator (e.g. predictTo from each queried current() caller that knows the current data time).

findings.title: Bearing-bias sign convention documented backwards on the port: 'added to a camera's reported bearing' vs code that subtracts
findings.description: applyBiasCorrection subtracts the published bearing bias (out.value(0) -= bb.bias_rad, line 72; RangeBearing2D line 81), consistent with the estimator's model observed = true + b (SensorBiasEstimator.cpp:142-143: alpha_pred = atan2 + b_hat). But the port struct's doc says the opposite: ports/ISensorBiasProvider.hpp:44 — 'Bearing-bias snapshot (added to a camera's reported bearing, rad)'. A consumer hand-seeding FixedSensorBiasProvider::setBearingBias from an offline calibration and following the port comment will negate the sign, and applyBiasCorrection will then double the camera's bearing error instead of cancelling it. Sign errors here are exactly the 'worse than no correction' hazard: the corrupted bearing feeds association and the track position directly.
findings.failure_scenario: Camera reads 2 deg high. Consumer reads the port doc, reasons 'the stored value is added to the reported bearing to correct it' and stores bias_rad = -2 deg via FixedSensorBiasProvider (a documented deployment path, ISensorBiasProvider.hpp:94-106). applyBiasCorrection computes beta - (-2 deg) = beta + 2 deg: every camera bearing is now 4 deg off, silently, with a tight claimed variance (default 1e-8).
findings.evidence: core/pipeline/BiasCorrection.hpp:69-85 (subtraction, both Bearing2D and RangeBearing2D); ports/ISensorBiasProvider.hpp:44-49 ('added to'); core/bias/SensorBiasEstimator.cpp:142-143 (estimator convention: measured = true + b, so correction must subtract).
findings.suggested_fix: Fix the port comment to state the convention explicitly ('b is the offset the sensor ADDS to truth; applyBiasCorrection subtracts it') and add the same sentence to FixedSensorBiasProvider::setBearingBias and the integration guide's config table row.

units.coverage_notes: Scope read end-to-end: core/bias/{HeadingBiasEstimator.{hpp,cpp}, HeadingBiasObservations.hpp, AisArpaPairExtractor.{hpp,cpp}, SensorBiasEstimator.{hpp,cpp}, SensorBiasPairExtractor.{hpp,cpp}}, core/pipeline/BiasCorrection.hpp; cross-read for wiring/consumers: Tracker.{hpp,cpp}, MhtTracker.cpp (touch + correction sections), SourceTouchPopulate.hpp, Measurement.hpp, Track.hpp (SourceTouch), MeasurementBuilders.cpp (sensor_position_enu), ports/{IBearingInnovationSink, IHeadingBiasProvider, ISensorBiasProvider}.hpp, adapters/{arpa,eoir,own_ship} bias paths, core/benchmark/Sweep.cpp bias wiring, integration-guide §5, specs 2026-06-03/04/13, all tests in tests/bias plus tests/sim/{BusComparisonHelpers.hpp, test_bus_anchor_loss.cpp, test_bus_bias_estimator_sweep.cpp} and tests/pipeline/test_bias_correction_orthogonality.cpp. Checked CLEAN: (1) angle wrapping — every accepted update wraps its residual before applying (v1 wraps z and y; v2 wraps y; mhs kinds wrap measurement and residual; SensorBias bearing wraps r); downstream EKF residuals wrap bearings (MeasurementModels.cpp:94-96) so the un-rewrapped Bearing2D value after applyBiasCorrection is harmless. (2) Sign convention measured = true + b is consistent across all five heading observation kinds, both SensorBias observation types, and the subtraction in applyBiasCorrection (only the port DOC string contradicts — reported). (3) Random-walk q scaled linearly by dt in both estimators, per-key in SensorBiasEstimator; dt<=0 and out-of-order predicts are no-ops; nanos==0 first-call sentinel is benign even for t=0 streams. (4) Eigen usage: P=(I-K)*P products evaluate into temporaries (no aliasing bug); block<2,2> accesses guarded by rows/cols checks; JPDA weight scaling in Tracker's emitter preserves the G2 ratio as intended; G2's R<=0 harmless-reject documented and correct. (5) NaN containment: SensorBias S.inverse() can go NaN only via caller-misuse zero R at repeated timestamps (extractors always fill R via covWithFallback; P stays PD when R is PSD since P0=25I), and a NaN state can never publish (NaN comparisons fail the publish gates). (6) Cross-sensor extractor determinism (std::map emission order) verified; same-source_id exclusion and one-obs-per-key correlation reasoning are sound. Borderline items NOT reported as findings: AisArpaPairExtractor pairs the OLDEST in-window AIS and ARPA touches (not nearest-in-time), leaving an unmodeled time-misalignment bearing error v_t*dt/r (~1.5 deg with the 1.5 s window used by the anchor-loss test at 12 m/s / 700 m) absent from sigma_v2; Track::SourceTouch.covariance defaults to Identity so a never-populated covariance reads as sigma=1 m instead of triggering the 10 m fallback in sigmaFromCov2D/covWithFallback (reachable only if an empty-covariance measurement survives update); current() can spuriously unpublish if an accepted observation is older than last_predict_ (last_update_ rewinds); wrapToPi boundary conventions differ between the two files ((-pi,pi] vs [-pi,pi]) — immaterial for scalar residuals; v1 path has no bi_min_range_m gate but is self-limited by sigma_v2 growth at short range. Known/by-design items excluded per instructions. Adjacent prior-round findings (PMBM spurious SourceTouches polluting these extractors — B2; RangeBearing2D initiate — B1) were confirmed as distinct and not re-reported; the PositionVelocity2D applyBiasCorrection skip overlaps a prior LOW/unverified entry and is marked as such. Main caveat on the three high findings: they are composition defects (estimator + extractor + tracker/adapter correction all individually defensible; the loop is what is wrong), so the fix owner is a design decision — raw-vs-corrected SourceTouch convention must be picked once and enforced.
